abstract: Psychological maturity for marriage remains a critical predictor for the stability and quality of future family life, particularly in an era of shifting social attitudes toward marital permanence. This study examines the relationship between psychological maturity for marriage and emotional intelligence among young men. To investigate this link, a study was conducted with 90 men aged 21 to 26, using the Psychological Maturity for Marriage Scale (SKALDOM II) and the Emotional Intelligence Questionnaire (INTE). Psychological maturity was assessed across three dimensions: self-acceptance, internal integration and world view, and attitudes toward marriage and family life. Comparative analysis of groups with high and low maturity scores revealed that men with higher psychological maturity possess significantly higher levels of emotional intelligence. Specifically, these individuals demonstrate a superior capacity for self-awareness, empathy, and the constructive regulation of emotions during problem-solving and social interactions. The findings suggest that emotional intelligence serves as a foundational competency for building resilient interpersonal bonds. The paper concludes by emphasizing the value of fostering emotional competencies through educational and family-oriented programs to better prepare young men for the complexities of modern marital and family life.
